title: Higher Loan, Higher Cost
fullRewardHint: Answer all {{total}} choices correctly to earn the full reward.
reflectionPrompts: How can you calculate the true cost difference between loan sizes before borrowing?
What strategies help you determine the minimum loan amount needed for your specific situation?

options.label: Total interest usually increases proportionally
options.reflection: Exactly! Bigger loans mean higher principal amounts, resulting in more total interest paid over the life of the loan.

options.label: Interest stays the same regardless of loan size
options.reflection: Actually, larger loans typically generate more total interest because you're paying interest on a bigger principal amount over time.

options.label: Interest rates decrease for larger loans
options.reflection: While some large loans may have better rates, the total interest cost still increases because you're borrowing more money overall.

options.label: Interest depends entirely on negotiation skills
options.reflection: Negotiation can help, but loan size fundamentally affects total interest - larger amounts mean more money subject to interest charges.

stages.prompt: Why does borrowing more create higher costs?

options.label: Larger loans are more administratively complex
options.reflection: Administrative complexity affects processing, but the core reason is mathematical - more principal means more interest accumulates over time.

options.label: Lenders perceive bigger loans as higher risk
options.reflection: Risk perception affects interest rates, but even with the same rate, larger principals generate more total interest dollars.

options.label: Large loans typically have longer repayment terms
options.reflection: Extended terms are common but not universal. The fundamental issue is that more borrowed money equals more interest regardless of term length.

options.label: More principal amount means more interest accumulation
options.reflection: Correct! Interest is calculated on the outstanding principal, so larger loans have more money accruing interest throughout the repayment period.

stages.prompt: How does loan size affect monthly payments?

options.label: Monthly payments stay the same regardless of loan amount
options.reflection: Monthly payments typically increase with loan size because you're repaying more principal plus the corresponding interest charges.

options.label: The difference is negligible for small loan increases
options.reflection: Even small increases compound over time. Each additional rupee borrowed adds to both immediate payments and total interest costs.

options.label: Payments increase proportionally with borrowed amount
options.reflection: Perfect! Larger loans require higher monthly payments to repay both the increased principal and accumulated interest within the same timeframe.

options.label: You can always adjust payments based on affordability
options.reflection: Payment flexibility varies by lender terms. The mathematical reality is that larger loans require larger payments to maintain the same repayment schedule.

stages.prompt: What's the relationship between loan size and total repayment?

options.label: Total repayment increases linearly with loan size
options.reflection: Close! Total repayment typically increases more than linearly because interest compounds on the larger principal amount over time.

options.label: Larger loans create exponentially higher total costs
options.reflection: Exactly! The combination of larger principal and compound interest creates disproportionately higher total repayment amounts for bigger loans.

options.label: Smaller loans actually cost more in total
options.reflection: This is backwards - smaller loans cost less overall. The principle is straightforward: borrow less, pay less in total interest and principal.

options.label: Loan size and total cost are unrelated factors
options.reflection: These are directly related - loan size is the foundation for calculating all interest charges and determines your total repayment obligation.

stages.prompt: What's the golden rule for loan sizing?

options.label: Borrow only what you truly need and can repay
options.reflection: Perfect! This principle minimizes both your monthly payment burden and total interest costs over the life of the loan.

options.label: Borrow the maximum amount you're approved for
options.reflection: Maximum borrowing creates maximum debt. The wise approach is borrowing only what you actually need and can comfortably repay.

options.label: Consider future needs when determining loan size
options.reflection: Future planning is important, but borrowing for anticipated needs often leads to unnecessary debt. Stick to current, verified requirements.

options.label: Match what others are borrowing for similar purposes
options.reflection: Others' borrowing decisions don't reflect your financial situation. Base loan amounts on your actual needs and repayment capacity.
